title: Time Crystals in Coupled Exciton-Polariton Condensates
authors: Xuan Ye, Hong-Jin Xiong, Alexey Kavokin, Sanjib Ghosh
abstract: In this paper, we show that time crystals can emerge in coupled exciton-polariton condensates without periodic external driving, enabled instead by incoherent gain and dissipation channels inherent to semiconductor microcavities. We present a full quantum description of these processes that recovers the established effective theory at the mean-field level. We analytically determine the mean-field phase diagram for the time-crystalline phase and find that its emergence requires the ratio of Kerr nonlinearity to nonlinear dissipation to exceed $\sqrt{5/4}$. Within this regime, the periodic oscillation of the particle numbers forms an attractor that is insensitive to the initial conditions. Numerical bifurcation diagrams reveal transitions between the time-crystalline phase and various steady phases, in excellent agreement with the analytical results. Using Bogoliubov perturbation theory, we evaluate the leading-order quantum corrections and find that, over a wide parameter range, these corrections remain periodic and much smaller than the mean-field background, thereby establishing the robustness of the time crystal.
summary: This paper shows that time crystals can spontaneously emerge in coupled exciton-polariton condensates using only the inherent gain and loss of the microcavity. It provides a theoretical threshold for this phase based on nonlinearity ratios and proves that the resulting oscillations are robust against quantum noise. This discovery offers a new way to realize time-crystalline behavior without the need for complex external driving.
main_problem: Investigating whether a time crystal phase can emerge in coupled exciton-polariton condensates without periodic external driving and determining its robustness against quantum fluctuations.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that time crystals can emerge via time-independent gain and dissipation channels, provided the ratio of Kerr nonlinearity to nonlinear dissipation exceeds sqrt(5/4), and show that this phase is robust against leading-order quantum corrections.
method: The study uses a Lindblad master equation approach, mean-field analysis via coupled Stuart-Landau equations, Jacobian matrix stability analysis, and Bogoliubov perturbation theory to evaluate quantum fluctuations.
model_or_system: The system consists of coupled exciton-polariton condensates in semiconductor microcavities, modeled by a Lindblad master equation including local linear gain, dissipation, nonlinear dissipation, and correlated gain/dissipating channels via a shared reservoir.
important_parameters: Nonlinearity ratio (alpha = eta_R/eta_I), gain/pumping parameter (beta = p/J_R), coupling parameter (gamma = J_I/J_R), and the regime of gain-dominated systems (p > 0, J_I > 0).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a mean-field approximation for the underlying dynamics and relies on the validity of Bogoliubov perturbation theory where quantum corrections remain small.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the microcavity setup; Figure 2 illustrates periodic oscillations and steady-state convergence; Figure 3 presents the phase diagram in the (gamma, beta) plane; Figure 4 compares stable versus divergent quantum fluctuations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of driving-free time crystals, describes the microcavity model and master equation, presents mean-field stability analysis and phase diagrams, evaluates quantum corrections using Bogoliubov theory, and concludes with a discussion on the robustness of the phase.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it identifies a novel mechanism for spontaneous time-translation symmetry breaking driven by intrinsic dissipation rather than external periodic driving.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic of the coupled microcavity setup. Two microcavity exciton-polariton condensates are coupled to a common exciton reservoir. Each condensate undergoes local linear gain and dissipation at rates g and κ, respectively, alongside nonlinear loss at rate ηI. The shared reservoir mediates correlated-incoherent gain and dissipation with rates JIg and JIl, while coherent quantum tunneling between the cavities occurs at rate JR.

Figure 2: Time-crystalline and steady states of the system. (a) In the time-crystalline phase, the particle numbers oscillate persistently in the long-time regime for a representative parameter set P/κ0 = (2, 3 × 10−4, 10−4, 1, 1). (b) Steady-state behavior outside the time- crystalline region. For parameter set P/κ0 = (2, 3 × 10−4, 10−4, 1, 0.6), both particle num- bers converge to a common value, representing a symmetric steady state. In contrast, for P/κ0 = (0.5, 3 × 10−4, 10−4, 1, 1), the populations converge to different values. (c) Fixed points determined by Eqs. (5) for P/κ0 = (4, 3×10−4, 3.2×10−4, 2, 1). The initial conditions Ri = 10−3

Figure 3: The phase diagram. (a) The green shaded region in the (γ, β) plane identifies the stable time-crystalline phase, with its boundary defined by the intersection of the instability thresholds for the in-phase symmetric (black), anti-phase symmetric (blue), and asymmetric(red) fixed points. (b) Bifurcation diagram versus γ for (p, ηR, ηI, JR)/κ0 = (2, 3×10−4, 10−4, 1), with JI/κ0 ∈[0.5, 2.0] along the horizontal purple line in (a). (c) Bifurcation diagram versus β for (ηR, ηI, JR, JI)/κ0 = (3 × 10−4, 10−4, 1, 1), with p/κ0 ∈[0.2, 4] along the vertical purple line in (a).

Figure 4: Robustness against quantum fluctuations. (a) Green region: time-crystalline phase determined from the mean-field equations. Red dots: parameter sets yielding stable quantum oscillations. Blue squares: parameter sets for which the quantum fluctuations grow exponentially, indicating the breakdown of perturbation theory. (b) Stable quantum oscillations for (ηR, ηI, JR, ω, gIg, gIl, g, κ)/κ0 = (3 × 10−4, 10−4, 1, 104, 3, 1, 3, 1). (c) Exponentially growing quantum fluctuations for (ηR, ηI, JR, ω, gIg, gIl, g, κ)/κ0 = (3×10−4, 10−4, 10, 104, 30, 10, 30, 10).
